question_title: Will Ekrem İmamoğlu be released from pre-trial detention in the 'political espionage' case before July 9, 2026?
summary: This is a status-quo-NO question. The Istanbul 25th High Criminal Court has ordered continuation of pre-trial detention at every hearing in the espionage case, rejecting all release requests. At the most recent hearing the prosecutor requested 15-20 year sentences and continued detention, which the court accepted, adjourning to July 6, 2026 \u2014 the only realistic decision point within the June 27\u2013July 9 window. The prosecution is part of a documented, politically motivated campaign against Erdogan's main rival, where Turkish courts consistently side with the government. A surprise release would be highly out of character for this court's established pattern. I retain a small residual probability (~4%) for an unexpected release under international pressure or a judicial-control substitution, slightly above the bare factor product to account for unmodeled tail scenarios. Premortem returned 'warn,' confirming the probability is correct.

factors.description: Court has repeatedly ordered continuation of pre-trial detention at every hearing in this case, rejecting all release requests — entrenched status quo

At the most recent hearing the prosecutor requested 15-20yr sentences and continued detention; the court accepted and adjourned to July 6 — a fresh signal pointing strongly to continued detention at the only in-window decision point
Prosecution widely documented as a politically motivated campaign against Erdogan's leading rival; courts consistently side with the government — strong political incentive against release

Small chance the July 6 hearing produces a surprise release/judicial-control substitution under international (ECtHR/EU) and domestic pressure, or because stated evidence-collection justification lapses

reasoning:
**Forecast: 4% (NO likely)**

İmamoğlu is very unlikely to be released from pre-trial detention in the espionage case before July 9, 2026. The court has an entrenched pattern of continuing his detention at every hearing, the prosecutor just secured a continuation order while requesting 15–20 year sentences, and the only in-window decision point (the July 6 hearing) is overwhelmingly likely to maintain detention given the documented political nature of the prosecution. I assign a small residual ~4% for a surprise release under international pressure or a judicial-control substitution.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, at any point between June 27, 2026 and July 9, 2026 (inclusive), a Turkish court orders the release of Ekrem İmamoğlu from pre-trial detention in the "political espionage" case (Istanbul 25th High Criminal Court, charges under Turkish Penal Code Article 328 relating to alleged sharing of voter/municipal data), such that he would no longer be held in custody on the basis of that case. This includes release on judicial control measures (e.g., house arrest, travel ban, or supervision) in lieu of detention in the espionage case. The question resolves based on reporting from the Media and Law Studies Association (MLSA, https://www.mlsaturkey.com/en/) or other [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions).
fine_print: "Release" refers specifically to a court decision lifting pre-trial detention in the espionage case (whether unconditionally or by substituting judicial control measures). If the court orders continuation of detention, or if no release decision is reported by July 9, 2026, the question resolves NO. The question resolves YES even if İmamoğlu would remain physically imprisoned due to his separate corruption case detention — what matters is whether a release/de-detention order is issued in the espionage case specifically. A conviction with a custodial sentence, or conversion of pre-trial detention into a post-conviction sentence, does not count as a release. If credible sources conflict, MLSA's reporting is authoritative.

factors.description: Court has repeatedly ordered continuation of pre-trial detention at every hearing in this case, rejecting all release requests — strong status quo
At most recent hearing prosecutor requested 15-20yr sentences and continued detention; court accepted and adjourned to July 6 — fresh signal pointing to continued detention
Case widely described as politically motivated against Erdogan's main rival; strong political incentive against release
Small chance July 6 hearing produces a surprise release (judicial control substitution) given international pressure

input.anchor: Base rate for release of a high-profile politically-detained defendant whose court has repeatedly refused release at consecutive hearings: very low, ~5%
input.assessment: This is a status-quo-NO question. The court has an established pattern of continuing detention at every hearing, the prosecutor just requested continued detention which the court accepted, and the political context strongly disfavors release. The only in-window decision point is the July 6 hearing. A surprise release would be highly newsworthy and out of character for this court's pattern. Probability of release ~3-5%.
input.calibration_notes: Status quo strongly favors NO. Not hedging toward 50% — evidence genuinely points to ~4%. Floor of 0.01-0.99 respected.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether July 6 hearing produces an unexpected release decision under domestic/international pressure. No indication of this in available info.
input.tool_audit: No external tools used — the question description provides sufficient detail (recent hearing outcome, prosecutor's request, adjournment to July 6). The situation is well-specified and recent (late June 2026).
input.process_reflection: For a clear status-quo question with a detailed description, heavy research delegation would be wasteful. The scaffolding handles this fine; the main value is the discipline of the premortem gate. No friction.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
I read the full trace (trace_at_premortem.md), the reflection.yaml, and searched the worldview store. The agent did ZERO external research — tool metrics confirm only 1 reflection call, and the trace contains no tool use. All four factors rest on facts the agent attributes to "the question description."

KEY ISSUE — unverified specifics in the strongest fresh-signal factor. The resolution criteria/fine print actually provided describe only the legal mechanism (Article 328, Istanbul 25th High Criminal Court, judicial-control substitution counts as YES). They do NOT contain the concrete claims the factors treat as established fact:
- Factor 1: "Court has repeatedly ordered continuation... rejecting all release requests" — not in the criteria given.
- Factor 2 (logit -1.5, the "fresh signal"): "prosecutor requested 15-20yr sentences and continued detention; court accepted and adjourned to July 6" — not in the criteria given, and unsourced.
- Factor 4: the "July 6 hearing" date — not in the criteria given.
Either the agent saw a fuller question body (a Metaculus background section, plausible) or it hallucinated the July 6 date and sentence figures. The trace doesn't reproduce the body and the agent did no corroborating research, so I can't confirm.

WHY THIS IS WARN, NOT FAIL — the core forecast direction is independently well-grounded. Worldview research (chp-ozgur-ozel...json, fresh) corroborates the load-bearing context: İmamoğlu detained since March 2025, the prosecution is part of a documented AKP/judiciary campaign against the opposition where courts consistently side with the government. This solidly supports Factor 3 and the dominant status-quo factor. The base rate for releasing a high-profile politically-detained opposition figure in a ~12-day window is genuinely very low. So even if Factor 2's specifics are wrong, the forecast (~4%) does not change. My independent estimate is ~3-5%, consistent with the agent's 4%. The probability is correct.

SECONDARY ISSUES:
- No research on a fast-moving, time-sensitive legal question. The question names MLSA as authoritative; a single MLSA/news check would have verified the unsourced Factor 2 claims and confirmed no release had already occurred. The tool_audit rationale ("description provides sufficient detail") is weak for this question type.
- Confidence 0.85 is mildly overconfident given the agent verified nothing independently and its fresh signal is unconfirmed.
- Resolution-criteria engagement is otherwise good: factors correctly scope to the espionage case specifically and note judicial-control substitution counts as YES.
- Tool_audit claim "No external tools used" matches ground-truth metrics (1 reflection call, 0 errors) — no discrepancy.
- Counterargument is substantive (ECtHR pressure, face-saving release, detention-time limits), not a token gesture.

Net: probability is right and direction is well-supported, but the agent leaned on unverified case-specific details while doing no research on a fast-moving situation, and was slightly overconfident.
